title: Scampi
subtitle: Breaded langoustine or king prawn tails, shallow-fried
excerpt: Langoustine tails or large king prawns coated in seasoned breadcrumbs and shallow-fried until crisp and golden. The proper pub scampi, made with whole shellfish rather than processed minced prawn.
sourceNotes: Scampi as a breaded and fried dish became popular in British pubs and restaurants in the 1950s and 1960s. The name refers to Nephrops norvegicus (langoustine or Norway lobster), though the dish is now more commonly made with prawns in commercial versions. The original scampi used whole langoustine tails, a tradition documented in British seafood cooking of the period.

content.text: Proper scampi is made with langoustine tails, not the minced and reformed prawn product that fills most pub baskets. If langoustines are unavailable, large raw king prawn tails are an excellent alternative. The breadcrumbing technique is the same as for goujons: flour, egg, panko, pressed on firmly. The frying time is short, prawns and langoustines need only 2 to 3 minutes.

Tartar sauce is the correct accompaniment. Make it fresh: shop-bought tartar sauce is too sweet and lacks the sharpness of capers and the texture of chopped gherkins. A lemon wedge alongside is non-negotiable.

items.name: Langoustine tails
items.prepNote: or large raw king prawn tails, peeled and deveined

content.text: Combine the mayonnaise, roughly chopped capers, finely chopped gherkins, parsley, and lemon juice. Season with salt and pepper. Taste and adjust. It should be sharp and herby, not sweet. Set aside.

Pat {{langoustine-tails}} langoustine or prawn tails dry with kitchen paper. Season the panko with smoked paprika if using. Set up three shallow bowls: seasoned flour, beaten egg, and seasoned panko. Coat each tail in flour, egg, then panko, pressing the crumb on firmly.

Heat sunflower oil to a depth of 1.5 cm in a frying pan over medium-high heat. When a crumb dropped in sizzles immediately, add the scampi in batches without crowding. Fry for 2 minutes on the first side, turn, and fry for 1 to 2 minutes more until golden and cooked through. Drain on kitchen paper. Serve immediately with the tartar sauce and lemon wedges.

items.fix: Prawns need only 3 to 4 minutes total. They are cooked when they turn pink and the flesh is just opaque. Remove immediately.
Pat the tails very dry before crumbing. Press the panko on with your palm. Let the crumbed scampi sit for a few minutes before frying.
items.cause: Crumb not pressed on firmly, or too much water on the shellfish

items.fix: The oil should sizzle immediately and visibly around the scampi when they go in. If it does not, remove them and wait for the oil to reach temperature.
Add another squeeze of lemon and taste. The tartar sauce should taste sharp and distinctly herby.
items.cause: Insufficient acid, needs more lemon juice or capers

content.text: Scampi became a staple of the British pub in the 1950s and 1960s. The Norway lobster (also called Dublin Bay prawn) was being landed in large numbers from British and Irish waters and needed a commercial use. The breaded and fried format, borrowed from Italian fritto misto di mare, suited a pub kitchen and grew very popular. As langoustine catches became more expensive, the pub version moved to minced prawn products. The homemade version, made with whole tails, is a different and far better dish.
